Santiago de Chile, Chile
O ct 6- 1 0, 20 19
This TripBook was produced for the 12th Americas Competitiveness Exchange program in Chile with valuable 
contributions from Rodrigo Del Canto, Daniela Sánchez, and César Carrasco, of the Universidad del Desarrollo 
(UDD) of Chile, and Francisca Cruzat, Director of International Affairs of CORFO, Chile.
The TripBook was developed by the RIAC Technical Secretariat of the Organization of American States (OAS) 
to support the engagement of ACE 12 leaders. The document was prepared by the OAS team under the 
guidance of Adriana Bonilla, ACE Program Coordinator, Cristina Narváez and Juan Sebastián Fonseca, who was 
responsible for the design of this document.

OAS’ Welcome Message
It is with great pleasure that I welcome you to the Twelfth Americas Competitiveness 
Exchange on Innovation and Entrepreneurship (ACE) in Chile, a country that has built a robust 
and globally recognized innovation ecosystem and nurtured a vibrant entrepreneurial culture.
You were selected from a highly competitive pool of global and regional leaders to participate 
in the ACE program, which is designed to share good practices, connect talent and build 
networks, geared towards generating opportunities for exploring and expanding the links 
between our economies.
ACE plays a critical role in strengthening hemispheric and global ties by building on ideas 
and models that work, in order to create jobs for our citizens. Since 2014, ACE has been 
an important vehicle for advancing commerce and trade between participating economies, 
accelerating business prospects, and promoting collaboration among stakeholders.
We are deeply grateful to the Government of Chile, CORFO, and the University of Development 
of Chile (UDD) for serving as host of ACE 12. We appreciate the engagement of leaders and 
stakeholders from Chile and their willingness to build stronger collaboration and long-lasting 
partnerships with OAS member states around their globally recognized innovation and 
entrepreneurship ecosystem.
Our special thanks to the Government of the United States particularly the U.S. Department 
of Commerce and the U.S. Department of State for their unwavering support to the ACE 
program since its inception.
We believe that by working together we can realize a more prosperous and resilient Hemisphere
 
Kim Osborne 
Executive Secretary for Integral Development 
Organization of American States
Kim Osborne
Executive Secretary for Integral Development 
Organization of American States
Dear ACE participants,

3
CORFO’s Welcome Message
The Chilean Government, through the Chilean Economic Development Agency (CORFO), 
would like to express its gratitude for the opportunity to host ACE 12.
CORFO is the Chilean Innovation and Economic Development Agency that promotes 
entrepreneurship, innovation, technological capabilities, competitiveness, and human 
capital. 
Chile is a country with a population of 18.5 million people with a successful economic 
development track record. Its trade policies have fostered a competitive and open economy, 
oriented towards free markets. As such, Chile has signed over 25 trade agreements with 64 
world economies, covering nearly 90% of international trade.
Among the main assets of Chile are its amazing tourist attractions, which led to the 
recognition of Chile as the World’s Leading Adventure Tourism Destination 2018 by the 
World Travel Awards. Chile’s economy is mainly based on natural resources. The country is 
a global leader on the copper and lithium mining industries, agriculture, and aquaculture. 
 
As a country, we are currently focused on raising awareness of climate change and enhancing 
our programs to promote circular economy as well as those projects which have triple 
impact: economic, social, and environmental.
We are also pursuing important policies to develop high added value clean energies and to 
encourage projects that can spur development for the local economy.
In our region, Chile is now a TRENDSETTER. 
In terms of renewable energy, Chile generates 2/3 of the solar power in Latin America, and 
20% of the country’s total energy already comes from Non-Conventional Renewable Energy 
(NCRE) sources, such as wind and solar. 
Pablo Terrazas
Vice President, CORFO 
Ministry of Economy
Government of Chile

4
Chile offers the best FIBER OPTIC connectivity in the region; the Internet coverage is 
almost at a 100 percent penetration and the country has become a global leader in public 
electromobility with hundreds of electric buses, improving the life quality of Chileans.
What about the future of Chile? We aspire to be the first developed Latin American country 
in terms of competitiveness and innovation. The challenge to incorporate technologies and 
innovation is especially relevant for those countries that – like Chile – need to upgrade their 
heavy dependence on commodities.
During the last 30 years, Chile has strengthened its economy and its export capacity, and 
although this strategy has been successful, Chile has begun to move towards an economy that 
further incorporates technology, particularly clean technologies, promotes the diversification 
of its productive matrix, and prepares the country to face the Fourth Industrial Revolution. 
When addressing technology, President Piñera usually says that the country missed the First, 
the Second, and the Third Industrial Revolution, and it is our job as a society to make sure 
that Chile becomes the leader of the Fourth Industrial Revolution in Latin America.
So, what better initiative for our strategy than the Americas Competitiveness Exchange? We 
are convinced that innovation is crucial for the development and adaptation of Chilean SME’s 
and large companies, as well as to tackle the technological advances that are reshaping the 
way that the companies are doing business around the world.
I invite you to engage with the opportunities Chile has to offer in the innovation industry, and 
to link this growing network, called ACE, with the Chilean innovation ecosystem.
I encourage you to think about Chile as a partner for the development and expansion of all 
your ideas, and we invite all ACE leaders to get to know our innovation and entrepreneurship 
ecosystem and look for opportunities for business, investment and R&D collaboration.
Thank you very much and welcome to Chile!
Pablo Terrazas
Vice President, CORFO 
Ministry of Economy
Government of Chile

5
UDD’s Welcome Message
On behalf of the “Universidad del Desarrollo”, I welcome the distinguished participants 
from different parts of the Americas and other regions of the world, who will be part of this 
successful and intense program we have contributed to organize. I am pleased that our 
university is coordinating, along with CORFO, the OAS and RIAC, the Twelfth Edition of the 
Americas Competitiveness Exchange, which will focus on Digital Transformation and the 
challenges it imposes on universities, companies and to all of us, as a society in general.
At the present time, the debate on subjects such as Artificial Intelligence, Automation of 
Labor, Robotics, the regulation of these technologies and the way it has and will continue to 
influence our lives, has been set at the global stage.
In Chile we are not oblivious to these changes, and this is why our university considers that 
the technological, social and business disruption that the Digital Transformation is causing in 
the various productive areas, constitutes a great opportunity.
It is for that reason, dear participants, that I invite you to take advantage of the opportunities 
to be generated by ACE 12 in Chile. It is a unique space to get to know our Innovation 
and Entrepreneurship leaders and ecosystem firsthand. You will be able to experience the 
strong spirit of innovation and energy that permeates Chile; to connect with outstanding 
initiatives and to find new opportunities for collaboration, start new business connections, 
and strengthen long-term relationships between Chile and your countries.
I conclude by wishing you all a pleasant stay in our country during ACE 12 in Chile, and that 
you do succeed in your proposed objectives for this program. We are looking forward to 
your participation and to make sure that you return to your countries with great news and 
partnerships that can contribute to advancing the development of our region.
 
Federico Valdés
President 
Universidad del Desarrollo
Federico Valdés
President 
Universidad del Desarrollo

6
12 Editions since 2014
A little bit of history…
The Americas Competitiveness Exchange on Innovation and Entrepreneurship (ACE) was 
created in 2014 by the U.S. Department of Commerce, the U.S. Department of State and 
the Organization of American States (OAS) to showcase first-hand successful examples of 
entrepreneurship, innovation, strategic investments and public-private partnerships from 
a specific region/country within the Western Hemisphere that contribute to economic 
development at the local, regional, and national levels.
 
The ACE has become a flagship hemispheric initiative for the promotion of better innovation 
and entrepreneurship ecosystems in the Americas. It is one of the main initiatives of the Inter-
American Competitiveness Network (RIAC), in which the OAS serves as Technical Secretariat. 
 
ACE activities are selected to further advance commerce, trade and investment between 
participating economies, accelerate business opportunities, and promote collaboration 
among stakeholders.

7
The ACE is leading CHANGE
Created over 1,000 jobs in North Carolina by drawing attention to the Conover global 
textile research center;
A $10M program to support innovation research ventures between Brazil and Israel;
A textile entrepreneurship support center in Hidalgo, Mexico which was inspired by the 
Manufacturing Solution Center of Conover, North Carolina, United States;
“The Ithaka Center of Entrepreneurship and Innovation” at the Catholic University of 
Uruguay (UCU), which was modeled after U.S. innovation centers;
Haiti’s first Incubator Center for Technological Innovation (ALPHA Haiti) which was 
modeled after U.S. and Mexico incubators;
Created 50 jobs in Wisconsin by creating new trade networks with Argentina and 
Mexico;
Two Innovation and Entrepreneurship Centers at UNITEC Honduras University inspired 
by California’s best practices and models;
The “Seed Capital for Biotechnological Entrepreneurs” project at the National Agency 
for Research and Innovation (ANII) of Uruguay, which was based on the Economic 
Development Administration (EDA) programs;
 
The “Fondéate” program which supports early stage companies and raises awareness 
about the importance of intelligent financing in Cali, Colombia, which was inspired by 
innovation hubs in Texas;

9
The 
12th 
Americas 
Competitiveness 
Exchange takes place in Latin America’s 
Innovation Hub: Chile. 
Chile was ranked as the most competitive 
country in Latin America (WEF, 2018) and a 
top performer on innovation in the region 
(GII, 2019), as a result of public policies that 
have consistently promoted innovation and 
entrepreneurship for the last ten years. To 
enhance the impact of innovation policies, the 
Government of Chile through its Economic 
Development Agency (CORFO) has sought 
to promote projects that integrate economic, 
social, and environmental impacts. Nurturing 
a vibrant tech-based entrepreneurial culture 
has been key to boost business creation 
and advancing the digital transformation 
that is putting the country in the forefront of 
Information and Communication Technologies 
(ICT) penetration into the public, private, and 
academic efforts. 
 
Academia has been a core component of the 
innovation ecosystem since its inception. Chile 
has one of the best education systems in Latin 
America and has reached 97 percent of literacy 
countrywide. To promote Science, Technology, 
Engineering, and Math (STEM) Programs, 
CORFO has implemented public policies to 
support universities in developing strategic 
plans to boost innovation, entrepreneurship, 
applied research, and technology transfer 
initiatives. Building on Chile’s robust scheme 
of business incubators and accelerators, 
CORFO has also promoted co-working 
spaces, mentorship networks, and incentives 
for risk capital investment to catalyze business 
creation and diversify the economy. 
Moreover, Chile has implemented a strategic 
policy to preserve natural resources and 
promote sustainable economic development, 
particularly in the energy sector. Thanks to 
its efforts in promoting sustainable energies, 
Chile has developed an attractive market for 
clean energies investment. In fact, Chile was 
chosen as a world leader in renewable energies 
according to ClimateScope 2018, a country-
by-country index of investment conditions for 
clean energy in emerging markets.
Chile is on the Rise!

Start-up Chile – a world-class business accelerator
Digital Country Foundation – fostering the digital culture
Telefónica Chile – top-notch Telecom company
Chile Foundation – do tank for tech innovation and implementation
UC Davis Chile – advancing AgTech-based education
SOCIALAB – social innovation booster
Credit and Investment Bank (BCI) – tech-driven innovative bank
Hubtec Chile – tech and science transfer center
Why is Chile an Innovation Hub?
Top Innovation Sites & Projects
Chile has undergone a startup revolution that has resulted in a robust innovation 
ecosystem and an active entrepreneurial mindset;
 
Academia plays a key role: offering tailored programs to prepare the workforce for the 
future, creating state of the art labs, and offering STEM programs;
 
Chile has leveraged Information and Communication Technologies to advance tech-
based innovation;
 
Chile has a top five FinTech ecosystem in Latin America growing exponentially in the last 
18 months;
 
BioTech is a great business opportunity in Chile as one of the most promising emerging 
sectors;
 
Chile has boosted renewable energy sources to build an attractive market for clean 
energy.

Chilean Economic 
Development Agency 
CORFO
An agency acting under the direction 
of the Ministry of Economy, Promotion, 
and Tourism, in charge of advancing 
entrepreneurship, 
innovation, 
and 
competitiveness in the country in order 
to strengthen the workforce and their 
technological skills. Its main objective is to 
promote a prosperous society with better 
opportunities for all and to foster economic 
development.
Website: www.corfo.cl/sites/cpp/home
Twitter: @Corfo
Start-Up Chile
A business accelerator created by the 
Government 
of 
Chile, 
developed 
to 
attract 
high-level 
innovation-based 
entrepreneurship initiatives to the country. 
Currently, it is the top accelerator in Latin 
America, top 10 worldwide, and has one of 
the most diverse startup communities in the 
world. 
Website: www.startupchile.org
Twitter: @startupchile
Palacio de la Moneda 
It is the Presidential Palace and it serves 
as the functional headquarters of several 
ministries, including the Ministry of Science, 
Technology, Knowledge, and Innovation. 
La Moneda was built in 1805, originally 
intended to be a coin mint.
Website: https://www.gob.cl/
Twitter: @GobiernodeChile
Universidad del 
Desarrollo - UDD
 
A private university established in 1990 in 
Concepción, Chile, as part of a national 
educational project to promote excellency. 
Currently, is one of most prestigious 
universities in Chile, well known for its 
academic innovation and its collaboration 
with key local and international stakeholders 
to prepare the workforce for the future. 
Website: https://www.udd.cl/
Twitter: @UDD_cl
The 12th ACE Site Visits

18
Chilean Institute Rational 
Business Administration 
ICARE
A non-profit established in 1953 by 
stakeholders of several economic sectors, 
meant to promote business development 
in Chile. Its members are more than 1.200 
companies of different sizes and industry 
sectors. ICARE provides relevant data 
regarding local and international markets 
to facilitate the understanding and timely 
assessment 
of 
business 
management, 
policies, and practices. 
Website: https://www.icare.cl/
Twitter: @icare_chile
Telefónica Chile
One of the largest telecom companies 
in the country, providing a wide range of 
Information and Communication services 
to ensure connectivity. 
Website: http://www.telefonicachile.cl/
Twitter: @TelefonicaCL
Caja Los Andes
 
A non-profit compensation fund established 
in 1953 that aims at improving the life quality 
of its members, providing services on health, 
finance, tourism, education, and innovation.
Website:www.cajalosandes.cl/home_personas
Twitter: @CajaLosAndes
Endeavor Chile
 
With its headquarters located in New 
York, 
U.S., 
it 
is 
leading 
high-impact 
entrepreneurship 
in 
emerging 
markets 
worldwide. It selects, advice, and accelerates 
top high-impact entrepreneurs around the 
globe providing value-added services that 
help develop businesses and create jobs, 
transform economies, and support the 
future generation of businesspeople. 
Website: http://www.endeavor.cl/
Twitter: @endeavorchile 
Federico Santa María 
Technical University 
UTFSM
A technology-focused private university 
that seeks to promote knowledge sharing 
worldwide. Well known for its academic 
programs on Engineering, Science, and 
Technology. The Main Campus is located 
in Valparaíso and has other branches in 
Santiago, Viña del Mar, and Concepción.
Website: https://www.usm.cl/ 
Twitter: @usantamaria

19
Viñamar
Viñamar 
was 
founded 
to 
create 
an 
exceptional line of still and sparkling wines 
from the Casablanca Valley, an emblematic 
landmark of Chilean viticulture, well-known 
worldwide. Each and every wine is a tribute 
to the most prominent feature of the valley: 
its proximity to the sea. Thanks to the 
geographic features and the originality of the 
products, Viñamar’s wines excel in quality for 
the world’s most demanding markets. 
Website: http://www.vinamar.cl
Twitter: @vinamar_
Fundación Chile 
 
 
 
A non-profit created jointly in 1976 by the 
Government of Chile and BHP Billiton-
Minera Escondida. Chile Foundation is a do-
tank that forges high-impact innovation and 
enhances human capital to increase Chile’s 
competitiveness. Pioneer in opening new 
markets with a wide business portfolio of 
tech-driven companies. Its main areas are 
sustainability, agriculture, education, and 
entrepreneurship. 
Website: https://fch.cl/
Twitter: @fundacionchile
Banco de Crédito e 
Inversiones (BCI) - Centro 
NACE
 
A tech-driven innovative bank with presence 
in several countries of Latin America, 
the United States, and China. BCI has an 
entrepreneurship center, BCI Nace, which 
offers a comprehensive set of services to 
promote Latin America’s businesspeople 
to implement their ideas, develop their 
business, and scale up their companies. 
Website: www.bci.cl/empresarios/centro-nace 
Twitter: @BancoBci
Fundación la Protectora 
de la Infancia
A non-profit with over 120 years of 
experience in child protection services, 
implementing an innovative approach to 
improving their wellbeing. More than 1,300 
people work together in a network of 35 
social programs and academic institutions 
to build their skill sets and help them prepare 
for future challenges.
Website: https://www.protectora.cl/
Twitter: @Laprotectora
